hero_headline: Back to School: Set the AI Expectations Before Your Students Do
intro: Welcome back. AI did not take the summer off: Anthropic just made premium Claude free for teachers, the tools our district already uses shipped big updates, four more states ordered up district AI policies, and the research on what works and what backfires got a lot sharper. Here is what is worth knowing as students return.

editor_pick.title: Claude for Teachers: free premium AI for verified K-12 educators
editor_pick.blurb: This is the big one this month. Anthropic is giving verified US K-12 teachers free access to its premium Claude tier, with lesson planning mapped to academic standards in all 50 states, evidence-based curricula like Illustrative Mathematics built in, and connections to classroom tools like Diffit and Canva. Frontier-level AI at a price every school budget can love. Verify with your school email and see what it does for your planning time.

sections.name: Leading & Policy
sections.sub: For the people making the calls

items.title: 4 More States Require Districts to Adopt AI Policies
items.takeaway: Idaho, Maryland, Oklahoma, and Virginia just made district AI policies mandatory. The map is filling in, and Tennessee will not stay blank forever.

items.title: Creating 5 Pillars to Guide AI Use in Your District
items.takeaway: One district boiled its whole AI strategy down to five pillars you could sketch on a napkin. A ready-made starting template.

items.title: What School and District Leaders Need to Know Before They Invest in AI
items.takeaway: A pre-purchase checklist for AI contracts, including the question most districts forget to ask: who owns what students create in the tool?

items.title: At a U.S. Senate Hearing, a Call for AI That Protects Human Judgment in Schools
items.takeaway: Congress is asking the same question the faculty lounge is: how do we use AI without handing over the judgment calls that belong to educators?

items.title: The Missing Voices in School AI Conversations Are Closest to Learning: Teachers
items.takeaway: AI decisions made without teachers in the room tend to fail in the classroom. Worth reading before your next AI committee meeting.

items.title: The Sharper Question Is Whether Students Should Help Govern AI
items.takeaway: One district gave eighth graders voting seats on its AI policy committee, rules that bind every adult in the building. The case for asking students, not just about them.

items.title: Catholic Schools Are Partnering With Google on AI. Can They Protect Student Privacy?
items.takeaway: A whole school system signed on with Google for AI, and the privacy questions critics raised are the ones to ask before any vendor deal.

sections.name: In the Classroom
sections.sub: Teaching, learning, and the students themselves

items.title: Lessons on AI for Elementary Students: Teach Them Good Habits Now
items.takeaway: Elementary teachers are teaching AI habits the same way they teach hand-raising. Classroom-tested starters for K-5, perfect for week one.

items.title: 8 Classroom AI Policies Developed by Teachers
items.takeaway: Real AI policies written by real teachers, with templates you can adapt before students walk in. Setting the norm early beats retrofitting one in October.

items.title: In an AI Classroom, Content Knowledge Matters More Than Ever
items.takeaway: Your subject expertise is now the quality control layer nothing else can replace. A good reframe if AI has felt like a threat.

items.title: Giving Kids Access to AI Tutors Doesn't Mean They'll Use Them
items.takeaway: Students handed a free AI tutor barely logged in. The rollout plan matters more than the license.

items.title: How a Vinyl Record Resurgence Helped Me Understand the Future of AI in Education
items.takeaway: Why is vinyl booming in the streaming era? Same reason productive struggle still belongs in your classroom.

items.title: In the Rush to Adopt New AI Technologies, Let Us Not Forget the Human Touch
items.takeaway: A short reminder that the relationship between you and your students is still the technology that matters most.

items.title: High School Students Teach AI Literacy to Their Community
items.takeaway: Two high schoolers are teaching AI literacy to elementary kids and senior citizens. Your students might be your best AI ambassadors.

items.title: New Microsoft 365 Education Experiences Put Learning First
items.takeaway: We are a Microsoft district, so this lands in our own tenant: unit planning in Copilot, per-assignment student AI guidelines in Teams, and a Study and Learn agent that coaches instead of answers.

items.title: What's New for Teachers This Back to School
items.takeaway: MagicSchool's new-year release: a personalized homescreen, smarter search, and an assistant that routes you to the right tool in plain English. It is already in your launch pad.

items.title: What's New in Microsoft Education: Summer 2026 Updates for Teachers and Students
items.takeaway: A demo-driven walkthrough of the new Copilot education features from the editor's pick above. Watch it once and you are current.

items.title: ISTE 2026: Classroom Inside Gemini and New Study Notebooks
items.takeaway: Google's fall lineup. Useful context for what students may be using at home, even in a Microsoft district.

items.title: Turning Lesson Ideas Into Usable Resources With AI
items.takeaway: A second grade teacher's simple workflow for turning instructional ideas into ready-to-use materials. Easy to replicate during preplanning.

items.title: Sharpen AI Skills While Kids Learn New Words
items.takeaway: Four ready-to-use activities that teach vocabulary and AI skepticism in the same lesson. No extra prep unit required.

items.title: A Teacher Prize With Artificial Intelligence at the Center
items.takeaway: A New York teacher just won a major prize with AI at the heart of the story. Using AI well is becoming an award-worthy teaching skill.

sections.name: Proceed With Caution
sections.sub: The other side of the conversation
sections.note: A standing spot for the honest watch-outs. AI is a tool, not a shortcut around judgment, and these are the limits and risks worth keeping in front of us.

items.title: Teachers Save Time With AI. Their Students May Pay the Price
items.takeaway: The first randomized trial of AI in real classrooms: untrained AI use made classes less engaging and lowered scores where instruction was already weakest. Training first, tools second.

items.title: AI Changes Its Feedback on Students' Writing When It Knows Their Race and Gender
items.takeaway: Same essay, different feedback depending on the student's demographics. Read this before trusting AI to coach or grade writing.

items.title: The Canvas Breach: 275 Million Teachers and Students Exposed
items.takeaway: Hackers got in through free teacher accounts nobody vetted. A warning about every free ed-tech signup on your campus.

items.title: ChatGPT Knows Too Much: 8 Ways to Lock Down Your Privacy
items.takeaway: Ten minutes of settings changes that close most of the privacy leaks. Do this before you make ChatGPT your co-planner this year.

items.title: Anne Hathaway Rejected Every Candidate Who Sent a ChatGPT Thank-You Note
items.takeaway: Every finalist sent the identical AI-written note, and none of them got the job. Let AI draft if you like, but never let it replace your own voice.

items.title: AI in Schools Is Changing: What Leaders Need Now
items.takeaway: A reset for 2026-27: past the tips-and-tricks phase, into AI companions, student wellbeing, and shadow AI. A good framing episode before the year starts.

items.title: 4 Chatbot Strategies for Teachers Who Want Better Results From AI
items.takeaway: Four tool-agnostic prompting strategies you can use on day one of lesson prep. Short and immediately useful.

items.title: Finding the Students Schools Miss: How Data, Relationships, and AI Unlock Hidden Potential
items.takeaway: Data plus AI plus human judgment surfaced 2.5 times more students ready for advanced coursework. Your building already has them.

challenge.title: Write your classroom AI expectations in one paragraph before students arrive
challenge.body: Borrow from the teacher-written policies in this issue, keep it to a single paragraph, and post it where students will see it in week one. Then tell me what you settled on. The teachers in that Edutopia piece all landed on the same lesson: setting the norm early beats policing it later.
